title: Aeneid, Book 4
source: Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225, 4th-5th c.) via Vatican Library IIIF + Perseus Digital Library Latin (Greenough ed.) + Dryden English (1697, PG #228)
description: Book 4 of Virgil's Aeneid. Manuscript folio from Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225), the oldest surviving illustrated manuscript of Virgil (4th-5th century AD), held at the Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana. Latin text verbatim from the Greenough edition via Perseus Digital Library (public domain). English translation verbatim from John Dryden's verse rendering (1697, Project Gutenberg #228, public domain).
introduction:
This entry presents Book 4 of Virgil's Aeneid with the strict three-criteria standard of the Theosis Library:

  1. Manuscript scan: A folio from the Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225, 4th-5th century), one of the oldest and most important surviving Virgil manuscripts, with painted illustrations in the late antique Roman style. Via Vatican Library IIIF (digi.vatlib.it).
  2. Original text: The complete Latin text of Aeneid Book 4 (705 lines), verbatim from the Greenough edition via Perseus Digital Library (public domain).
  3. English translation: John Dryden's celebrated verse translation (1697), verbatim from Project Gutenberg #228 (public domain).

translation.original_text: <sup>1</sup>At regina gravi iamdudum saucia cura <sup>2</sup>volnus alit venis, et caeco carpitur igni. <sup>3</sup>Multa viri virtus animo, multusque recursat <sup>4</sup>gentis honos: haerent infixi pectore voltus <sup>5</sup>verbaque, nec placidam membris dat cura quietem. <sup>6</sup>Postera Phoebea lustrabat lampade terras, <sup>7</sup>umentemque Aurora polo dimoverat umbram, <sup>8</sup>cum sic unanimam adloquitur male sana sororem: <sup>9</sup>Anna soror, quae me suspensam insomnia terrent! <sup>10</sup>Quis novus hic nostris successit sedibus hospes, <sup>11</sup>quem sese ore ferens, quam forti pectore et armis! <sup>12</sup>Credo equidem, nec vana fides, genus esse deorum. <sup>13</sup>Degeneres animos timor arguit: heu, quibus ille <sup>14</sup>iactatus fatis! Quae bella exhausta canebat! <sup>15</sup>Si mihi non animo fixum immotumque sederet, <sup>16</sup>ne cui me vinclo vellem sociare iugali, <sup>17</sup>postquam primus amor deceptam morte fefellit; <sup>18</sup>si non pertaesum thalami taedaeque fuisset, <sup>19</sup>huic uni forsan potui succumbere culpae. <sup>20</sup>Anna, fatebor enim, miseri post fata Sychaei <sup>21</sup>coniugis et sparsos fraterna caede Penatis, <sup>22</sup>solus hic inflexit sensus, animumque labantem <sup>23</sup>impulit: adgnosco veteris vestigia flammae. <sup>24</sup>Sed mihi vel tellus optem prius ima dehiscat, <sup>25</sup>vel Pater omnipotens adigat me fulmine ad umbras,
translation.text: Dido discovers to her sister her passion for Aeneas, and her thoughts of marrying him. She prepares a hunting match for his entertainment. Juno, by Venus’ consent, raises a storm, which separates the hunters, and drives Aeneas and Dido into the same cave, where their marriage is supposed to be completed. Jupiter despatches Mercury to Aeneas, to warn him from Carthage. Aeneas secretly prepares for his voyage. Dido finds out his design, and, to put a stop to it, makes use of her own and her sister’s entreaties, and discovers all the variety of passions that are incident to a neglected lover. When nothing could prevail upon him, she contrives her own death, with which this book concludes.

translation.original_text: <sup>26</sup>pallentis umbras Erebi noctemque profundam, <sup>27</sup>ante, Pudor, quam te violo, aut tua iura resolvo. <sup>28</sup>Ille meos, primus qui me sibi iunxit, amores <sup>29</sup>abstulit; ille habeat secum servetque sepulchro. <sup>30</sup>Sic effata sinum lacrimis implevit obortis. <sup>31</sup>Anna refert: O luce magis dilecta sorori, <sup>32</sup>solane perpetua maerens carpere iuventa, <sup>33</sup>nec dulcis natos, Veneris nec praemia noris? <sup>34</sup>Id cinerem aut Manis credis curare sepultos? <sup>35</sup>Esto: aegram nulli quondam flexere mariti, <sup>36</sup>non Libyae, non ante Tyro; despectus Iarbas <sup>37</sup>ductoresque alii, quos Africa terra triumphis <sup>38</sup>dives alit: placitone etiam pugnabis amori? <sup>39</sup>Nec venit in mentem, quorum consederis arvis? <sup>40</sup>Hinc Gaetulae urbes, genus insuperabile bello, <sup>41</sup>et Numidae infreni cingunt et inhospita Syrtis; <sup>42</sup>hinc deserta siti regio, lateque furentes <sup>43</sup>Barcaei.  Quid bella Tyro surgentia dicam, <sup>44</sup>germanique minas? <sup>45</sup>Dis equidem auspicibus reor et Iunone secunda <sup>46</sup>hunc cursum Iliacas vento tenuisse carinas. <sup>47</sup>Quam tu urbem, soror, hanc cernes, quae surgere regna <sup>48</sup>coniugio tali! Teucrum comitantibus armis <sup>49</sup>Punica se quantis attollet gloria rebus! <sup>50</sup>Tu modo posce deos veniam, sacrisque litatis
translation.text:
But anxious cares already seiz’d the queen: She fed within her veins a flame unseen; The hero’s valour, acts, and birth inspire Her soul with love, and fan the secret fire. His words, his looks, imprinted in her heart, Improve the passion, and increase the smart. Now, when the purple morn had chas’d away The dewy shadows, and restor’d the day, Her sister first with early care she sought, And thus in mournful accents eas’d her thought:

“My dearest Anna, what new dreams affright My lab’ring soul! what visions of the night Disturb my quiet, and distract my breast With strange ideas of our Trojan guest! His worth, his actions, and majestic air, A man descended from the gods declare. Fear ever argues a degenerate kind; His birth is well asserted by his mind. Then, what he suffer’d, when by Fate betray’d! What brave attempts for falling Troy he made! Such were his looks, so gracefully he spoke, That, were I not resolv’d against the yoke Of hapless marriage, never to be curst With second love, so fatal was my first, To this one error I might yield again; For, since Sichaeus was untimely slain, This only man is able to subvert The fix’d foundations of my stubborn heart. And, to confess my frailty, to my shame, Somewhat I find within, if not the same, Too like the sparkles of my former flame. But first let yawning earth a passage rend, And let me thro’ the dark abyss descend; First let avenging Jove, with flames from high, Drive down this body to the nether sky, Condemn’d with ghosts in endless night to lie, Before I break the plighted faith I gave! No! he who had my vows shall ever have; For, whom I lov’d on earth, I worship in the grave.”

translation.original_text: <sup>51</sup>indulge hospitio, causasque innecte morandi, <sup>52</sup>dum pelago desaevit hiemps et aquosus Orion, <sup>53</sup>quassataeque rates, dum non tractabile caelum. <sup>54</sup>His dictis incensum animum inflammavit amore, <sup>55</sup>spemque dedit dubiae menti, solvitque pudorem. <sup>56</sup>Principio delubra adeunt, pacemque per aras <sup>57</sup>exquirunt; mactant lectas de more bidentis <sup>58</sup>legiferae Cereri Phoeboque patrique Lyaeo, <sup>59</sup>Iunoni ante omnis, cui vincla iugalia curae. <sup>60</sup>Ipsa, tenens dextra pateram, pulcherrima Dido <sup>61</sup>candentis vaccae media inter cornua fundit, <sup>62</sup>aut ante ora deum pinguis spatiatur ad aras, <sup>63</sup>instauratque diem donis, pecudumque reclusis <sup>64</sup>pectoribus inhians spirantia consulit exta. <sup>65</sup>Heu vatum ignarae mentes! quid vota furentem, <sup>66</sup>quid delubra iuvant? Est mollis flamma medullas <sup>67</sup>interea, et tacitum vivit sub pectore volnus. <sup>68</sup>Uritur infelix Dido, totaque vagatur <sup>69</sup>urbe furens, qualis coniecta cerva sagitta, <sup>70</sup>quam procul incautam nemora inter Cresia fixit <sup>71</sup>pastor agens telis, liquitque volatile ferrum <sup>72</sup>nescius; illa fuga silvas saltusque peragrat <sup>73</sup>Dictaeos; haeret lateri letalis arundo. <sup>74</sup>Nunc media Aenean secum per moenia ducit, <sup>75</sup>Sidoniasque ostentat opes urbemque paratam;
translation.text: She said: the tears ran gushing from her eyes, And stopp’d her speech. Her sister thus replies: “O dearer than the vital air I breathe, Will you to grief your blooming years bequeath, Condemn’d to waste in woes your lonely life, Without the joys of mother or of wife? Think you these tears, this pompous train of woe, Are known or valued by the ghosts below? I grant that, while your sorrows yet were green, It well became a woman, and a queen, The vows of Tyrian princes to neglect, To scorn Hyarbas, and his love reject, With all the Libyan lords of mighty name; But will you fight against a pleasing flame! This little spot of land, which Heav’n bestows, On ev’ry side is hemm’d with warlike foes; Gaetulian cities here are spread around, And fierce Numidians there your frontiers bound; Here lies a barren waste of thirsty land, And there the Syrtes raise the moving sand; Barcaean troops besiege the narrow shore, And from the sea Pygmalion threatens more. Propitious Heav’n, and gracious Juno, lead This wand’ring navy to your needful aid: How will your empire spread, your city rise, From such a union, and with such allies? Implore the favour of the pow’rs above, And leave the conduct of the rest to love. Continue still your hospitable way, And still invent occasions of their stay, Till storms and winter winds shall cease to threat, And planks and oars repair their shatter’d fleet.”

translation.original_text: <sup>76</sup>incipit effari, mediaque in voce resistit; <sup>77</sup>nunc eadem labente die convivia quaerit, <sup>78</sup>Iliacosque iterum demens audire labores <sup>79</sup>exposcit, pendetque iterum narrantis ab ore. <sup>80</sup>Post, ubi digressi, lumenque obscura vicissim <sup>81</sup>luna premit suadentque cadentia sidera somnos, <sup>82</sup>sola domo maeret vacua, stratisque relictis <sup>83</sup>incubat, illum absens absentem auditque videtque; <sup>84</sup>aut gremio Ascanium, genitoris imagine capta, <sup>85</sup>detinet, infandum si fallere possit amorem. <sup>86</sup>Non coeptae adsurgunt turres, non arma iuventus <sup>87</sup>exercet, portusve aut propugnacula bello <sup>88</sup>tuta parant; pendent opera interrupta, minaeque <sup>89</sup>murorum ingentes aequataque machina caelo. <sup>90</sup>Quam simul ac tali persensit peste teneri <sup>91</sup>cara Iovis coniunx, nec famam obstare furori, <sup>92</sup>talibus adgreditur Venerem Saturnia dictis: <sup>93</sup>Egregiam vero laudem et spolia ampla refertis <sup>94</sup>tuque puerque tuus, magnum et memorabile numen, <sup>95</sup>una dolo divom si femina victa duorum est! <sup>96</sup>Nec me adeo fallit veritam te moenia nostra <sup>97</sup>suspectas habuisse domos Karthaginis altae. <sup>98</sup>Sed quis erit modus, aut quo nunc certamine tanto? <sup>99</sup>Quin potius pacem aeternam pactosque hymenaeos <sup>100</sup>exercemus? Habes, tota quod mente petisti:
translation.text:
These words, which from a friend and sister came, With ease resolv’d the scruples of her fame, And added fury to the kindled flame. Inspir’d with hope, the project they pursue; On ev’ry altar sacrifice renew: A chosen ewe of two years old they pay To Ceres, Bacchus, and the God of Day; Preferring Juno’s pow’r, for Juno ties The nuptial knot and makes the marriage joys. The beauteous queen before her altar stands, And holds the golden goblet in her hands. A milk-white heifer she with flow’rs adorns, And pours the ruddy wine betwixt her horns; And, while the priests with pray’r the gods invoke, She feeds their altars with Sabaean smoke, With hourly care the sacrifice renews, And anxiously the panting entrails views. What priestly rites, alas! what pious art, What vows avail to cure a bleeding heart! A gentle fire she feeds within her veins, Where the soft god secure in silence reigns.

Sick with desire, and seeking him she loves, From street to street the raving Dido roves. So when the watchful shepherd, from the blind, Wounds with a random shaft the careless hind, Distracted with her pain she flies the woods, Bounds o’er the lawn, and seeks the silent floods, With fruitless care; for still the fatal dart Sticks in her side, and rankles in her heart. And now she leads the Trojan chief along The lofty walls, amidst the busy throng; Displays her Tyrian wealth, and rising town, Which love, without his labour, makes his own. This pomp she shows, to tempt her wand’ring guest; Her falt’ring tongue forbids to speak the rest. When day declines, and feasts renew the night, Still on his face she feeds her famish’d sight; She longs again to hear the prince relate His own adventures and the Trojan fate. He tells it o’er and o’er; but still in vain, For still she begs to hear it once again. The hearer on the speaker’s mouth depends, And thus the tragic story never ends.

translation.original_text: <sup>101</sup>ardet amans Dido, traxitque per ossa furorem. <sup>102</sup>Communem hunc ergo populum paribusque regamus <sup>103</sup>auspiciis; liceat Phrygio servire marito, <sup>104</sup>dotalisque tuae Tyrios permittere dextrae. <sup>105</sup>Olli—sensit enim simulata mente locutam, <sup>106</sup>quo regnum Italiae Libycas averteret oras— <sup>107</sup>sic contra est ingressa Venus: Quis talia demens <sup>108</sup>abnuat, aut tecum malit contendere bello, <sup>109</sup>si modo, quod memoras, factum fortuna sequatur. <sup>110</sup>Sed fatis incerta feror, si Iuppiter unam <sup>111</sup>esse velit Tyriis urbem Troiaque profectis, <sup>112</sup>miscerive probet populos, aut foedera iungi. <sup>113</sup>Tu coniunx tibi fas animum temptare precando. <sup>114</sup>Perge; sequar.  Tum sic excepit regia Iuno: <sup>115</sup>Mecum erit iste labor: nunc qua ratione, quod instat <sup>116</sup>confieri possit, paucis, adverte, docebo. <sup>117</sup>Venatum Aeneas unaque miserrima Dido <sup>118</sup>in nemus ire parant, ubi primos crastinus ortus <sup>119</sup>extulerit Titan, radiisque retexerit orbem. <sup>120</sup>His ego nigrantem commixta grandine nimbum, <sup>121</sup>dum trepidant alae, saltusque indagine cingunt, <sup>122</sup>desuper infundam, et tonitru caelum omne ciebo. <sup>123</sup>Diffugient comites et nocte tegentur opaca: <sup>124</sup>speluncam Dido dux et Troianus eandem <sup>125</sup>devenient; adero, et, tua si mihi certa voluntas,
translation.text: Then, when they part, when Phoebe’s paler light Withdraws, and falling stars to sleep invite, She last remains, when ev’ry guest is gone, Sits on the bed he press’d, and sighs alone; Absent, her absent hero sees and hears; Or in her bosom young Ascanius bears, And seeks the father’s image in the child, If love by likeness might be so beguil’d.

translation.original_text: <sup>126</sup>conubio iungam stabili propriamque dicabo, <sup>127</sup>hic hymenaeus erit.—Non adversata petenti <sup>128</sup>adnuit, atque dolis risit Cytherea repertis. <sup>129</sup>Oceanum interea surgens Aurora reliquit. <sup>130</sup>It portis iubare exorto delecta iuventus; <sup>131</sup>retia rara, plagae, lato venabula ferro, <sup>132</sup>Massylique ruunt equites et odora canum vis. <sup>133</sup>Reginam thalamo cunctantem ad limina primi <sup>134</sup>Poenorum exspectant, ostroque insignis et auro <sup>135</sup>stat sonipes, ac frena ferox spumantia mandit. <sup>136</sup>Tandem progreditur, magna stipante caterva, <sup>137</sup>Sidoniam picto chlamydem circumdata limbo. <sup>138</sup>Cui pharetra ex auro, crines nodantur in aurum, <sup>139</sup>aurea purpuream subnectit fibula vestem. <sup>140</sup>Nec non et Phrygii comites et laetus Iulus <sup>141</sup>incedunt. Ipse ante alios pulcherrimus omnis <sup>142</sup>infert se socium Aeneas atque agmina iungit. <sup>143</sup>Qualis ubi hibernam Lyciam Xanthique fluenta <sup>144</sup>deserit ac Delum maternam invisit Apollo, <sup>145</sup>instauratque choros, mixtique altaria circum <sup>146</sup>Cretesque Dryopesque fremunt pictique Agathyrsi; <sup>147</sup>ipse iugis Cynthi graditur, mollique fluentem <sup>148</sup>fronde premit crinem fingens atque implicat auro; <sup>149</sup>tela sonant umeris: haud illo segnior ibat <sup>150</sup>Aeneas; tantum egregio decus enitet ore.
translation.text:
Meantime the rising tow’rs are at a stand; No labours exercise the youthful band, Nor use of arts, nor toils of arms they know; The mole is left unfinish’d to the foe; The mounds, the works, the walls, neglected lie, Short of their promis’d heighth, that seem’d to threat the sky,

But when imperial Juno, from above, Saw Dido fetter’d in the chains of love, Hot with the venom which her veins inflam’d, And by no sense of shame to be reclaim’d, With soothing words to Venus she begun: “High praises, endless honours, you have won, And mighty trophies, with your worthy son! Two gods a silly woman have undone! Nor am I ignorant, you both suspect This rising city, which my hands erect: But shall celestial discord never cease? ’Tis better ended in a lasting peace. You stand possess’d of all your soul desir’d: Poor Dido with consuming love is fir’d. Your Trojan with my Tyrian let us join; So Dido shall be yours, Aeneas mine: One common kingdom, one united line. Eliza shall a Dardan lord obey, And lofty Carthage for a dow’r convey.” Then Venus, who her hidden fraud descried, Which would the scepter of the world misguide To Libyan shores, thus artfully replied: “Who, but a fool, would wars with Juno choose, And such alliance and such gifts refuse, If Fortune with our joint desires comply? The doubt is all from Jove and destiny; Lest he forbid, with absolute command, To mix the people in one common land. Or will the Trojan and the Tyrian line In lasting leagues and sure succession join? But you, the partner of his bed and throne, May move his mind; my wishes are your own.”

translation.original_text: <sup>151</sup>Postquam altos ventum in montis atque invia lustra, <sup>152</sup>ecce ferae, saxi deiectae vertice, caprae <sup>153</sup>decurrere iugis; alia de parte patentis <sup>154</sup>transmittunt cursu campos atque agmina cervi <sup>155</sup>pulverulenta fuga glomerant montisque relinquunt. <sup>156</sup>At puer Ascanius mediis in vallibus acri <sup>157</sup>gaudet equo, iamque hos cursu, iam praeterit illos, <sup>158</sup>spumantemque dari pecora inter inertia votis <sup>159</sup>optat aprum, aut fulvum descendere monte leonem. <sup>160</sup>Interea magno misceri murmure caelum <sup>161</sup>incipit; insequitur commixta grandine nimbus; <sup>162</sup>et Tyrii comites passim et Troiana iuventus <sup>163</sup>Dardaniusque nepos Veneris diversa per agros <sup>164</sup>tecta metu petiere; ruunt de montibus amnes. <sup>165</sup>Speluncam Dido dux et Troianus eandem <sup>166</sup>deveniunt: prima et Tellus et pronuba Iuno <sup>167</sup>dant signum; fulsere ignes et conscius aether <sup>168</sup>conubiis, summoque ulularunt vertice nymphae. <sup>169</sup>Ille dies primus leti primusque malorum <sup>170</sup>causa fuit; neque enim specie famave movetur, <sup>171</sup>nec iam furtivum Dido meditatur amorem: <sup>172</sup>coniugium vocat; hoc praetexit nomine culpam. <sup>173</sup>Extemplo Libyae magnas it Fama per urbes— <sup>174</sup>Fama, malum qua non aliud velocius ullum; <sup>175</sup>mobilitate viget, viresque adquirit eundo,
translation.text: “Mine,” said imperial Juno, “be the care; Time urges, now, to perfect this affair: Attend my counsel, and the secret share. When next the Sun his rising light displays, And gilds the world below with purple rays, The queen, Aeneas, and the Tyrian court Shall to the shady woods, for sylvan game, resort. There, while the huntsmen pitch their toils around, And cheerful horns from side to side resound, A pitchy cloud shall cover all the plain With hail, and thunder, and tempestuous rain; The fearful train shall take their speedy flight, Dispers’d, and all involv’d in gloomy night; One cave a grateful shelter shall afford To the fair princess and the Trojan lord. I will myself the bridal bed prepare, If you, to bless the nuptials, will be there: So shall their loves be crown’d with due delights, And Hymen shall be present at the rites.” The Queen of Love consents, and closely smiles At her vain project, and discover’d wiles.

translation.original_text: <sup>176</sup>parva metu primo, mox sese attollit in auras, <sup>177</sup>ingrediturque solo, et caput inter nubila condit. <sup>178</sup>Illam Terra parens, ira inritata deorum, <sup>179</sup>extremam (ut perhibent) Coeo Enceladoque sororem <sup>180</sup>progenuit, pedibus celerem et pernicibus alis, <sup>181</sup>monstrum horrendum, ingens, cui, quot sunt corpore plumae <sup>182</sup>tot vigiles oculi subter, mirabile dictu, <sup>183</sup>tot linguae, totidem ora sonant, tot subrigit aures. <sup>184</sup>Nocte volat caeli medio terraeque per umbram, <sup>185</sup>stridens, nec dulci declinat lumina somno; <sup>186</sup>luce sedet custos aut summi culmine tecti, <sup>187</sup>turribus aut altis, et magnas territat urbes; <sup>188</sup>tam ficti pravique tenax, quam nuntia veri. <sup>189</sup>Haec tum multiplici populos sermone replebat <sup>190</sup>gaudens, et pariter facta atque infecta canebat: <sup>191</sup>venisse Aenean, Troiano sanguine cretum, <sup>192</sup>cui se pulchra viro dignetur iungere Dido; <sup>193</sup>nunc hiemem inter se luxu, quam longa, fovere <sup>194</sup>regnorum immemores turpique cupidine captos. <sup>195</sup>Haec passim dea foeda virum diffundit in ora. <sup>196</sup>Protinus ad regem cursus detorquet Iarban, <sup>197</sup>incenditque animum dictis atque aggerat iras. <sup>198</sup>Hic Hammone satus, rapta Garamantide Nympha, <sup>199</sup>templa Iovi centum latis immania regnis, <sup>200</sup>centum aras posuit, vigilemque sacraverat ignem,
translation.text:
The rosy morn was risen from the main, And horns and hounds awake the princely train: They issue early thro’ the city gate, Where the more wakeful huntsmen ready wait, With nets, and toils, and darts, beside the force Of Spartan dogs, and swift Massylian horse. The Tyrian peers and officers of state For the slow queen in antechambers wait; Her lofty courser, in the court below, Who his majestic rider seems to know, Proud of his purple trappings, paws the ground, And champs the golden bit, and spreads the foam around. The queen at length appears; on either hand The brawny guards in martial order stand. A flow’r’d simar with golden fringe she wore, And at her back a golden quiver bore; Her flowing hair a golden caul restrains, A golden clasp the Tyrian robe sustains. Then young Ascanius, with a sprightly grace, Leads on the Trojan youth to view the chase. But far above the rest in beauty shines The great Aeneas, the troop he joins; Like fair Apollo, when he leaves the frost Of wint’ry Xanthus, and the Lycian coast, When to his native Delos he resorts, Ordains the dances, and renews the sports; Where painted Scythians, mix’d with Cretan bands, Before the joyful altars join their hands: Himself, on Cynthus walking, sees below The merry madness of the sacred show. Green wreaths of bays his length of hair inclose; A golden fillet binds his awful brows; His quiver sounds: not less the prince is seen In manly presence, or in lofty mien.

Now had they reach’d the hills, and storm’d the seat Of salvage beasts, in dens, their last retreat. The cry pursues the mountain goats: they bound From rock to rock, and keep the craggy ground; Quite otherwise the stags, a trembling train, In herds unsingled, scour the dusty plain, And a long chase in open view maintain. The glad Ascanius, as his courser guides, Spurs thro’ the vale, and these and those outrides. His horse’s flanks and sides are forc’d to feel The clanking lash, and goring of the steel. Impatiently he views the feeble prey, Wishing some nobler beast to cross his way, And rather would the tusky boar attend, Or see the tawny lion downward bend.

translation.original_text: <sup>201</sup>excubias divom aeternas, pecudumque cruore <sup>202</sup>pingue solum et variis florentia limina sertis. <sup>203</sup>Isque amens animi et rumore accensus amaro <sup>204</sup>dicitur ante aras media inter numina divom <sup>205</sup>multa Iovem manibus supplex orasse supinis: <sup>206</sup>Iuppiter omnipotens, cui nunc Maurusia pictis <sup>207</sup>gens epulata toris Lenaeum libat honorem, <sup>208</sup>aspicis haec, an te, genitor, cum fulmina torques, <sup>209</sup>nequiquam horremus, caecique in nubibus ignes <sup>210</sup>terrificant animos et inania murmura miscent? <sup>211</sup>Femina, quae nostris errans in finibus urbem <sup>212</sup>exiguam pretio posuit, cui litus arandum <sup>213</sup>cuique loci leges dedimus, conubia nostra <sup>214</sup>reppulit, ac dominum Aenean in regna recepit. <sup>215</sup>Et nunc ille Paris cum semiviro comitatu, <sup>216</sup>Maeonia mentum mitra crinemque madentem <sup>217</sup>subnexus, rapto potitur: nos munera templis <sup>218</sup>quippe tuis ferimus, famamque fovemus inanem. <sup>219</sup>Talibus orantem dictis arasque tenentem <sup>220</sup>audiit omnipotens, oculosque ad moenia torsit <sup>221</sup>regia et oblitos famae melioris amantes. <sup>222</sup>Tum sic Mercurium adloquitur ac talia mandat: <sup>223</sup>Vade age, nate, voca Zephyros et labere pennis, <sup>224</sup>Dardaniumque ducem, Tyria Karthagine qui nunc <sup>225</sup>exspectat, fatisque datas non respicit urbes,
translation.text:
Meantime, the gath’ring clouds obscure the skies: From pole to pole the forky lightning flies; The rattling thunders roll; and Juno pours A wintry deluge down, and sounding show’rs. The company, dispers’d, to converts ride, And seek the homely cots, or mountain’s hollow side. The rapid rains, descending from the hills, To rolling torrents raise the creeping rills. The queen and prince, as love or fortune guides, One common cavern in her bosom hides. Then first the trembling earth the signal gave, And flashing fires enlighten all the cave; Hell from below, and Juno from above, And howling nymphs, were conscious of their love. From this ill-omen’d hour in time arose Debate and death, and all succeeding woes.

The queen, whom sense of honour could not move, No longer made a secret of her love, But call’d it marriage, by that specious name To veil the crime and sanctify the shame.

translation.original_text: <sup>226</sup>adloquere, et celeris defer mea dicta per auras. <sup>227</sup>Non illum nobis genetrix pulcherrima talem <sup>228</sup>promisit, Graiumque ideo bis vindicat armis; <sup>229</sup>sed fore, qui gravidam imperiis belloque frementem <sup>230</sup>Italiam regeret, genus alto a sanguine Teucri <sup>231</sup>proderet, ac totum sub leges mitteret orbem. <sup>232</sup>Si nulla accendit tantarum gloria rerum, <sup>233</sup>nec super ipse sua molitur laude laborem, <sup>234</sup>Ascanione pater Romanas invidet arces? <sup>235</sup>Quid struit, aut qua spe inimica in gente moratur, <sup>236</sup>nec prolem Ausoniam et Lavinia respicit arva? <sup>237</sup>Naviget: haec summa est; hic nostri nuntius esto. <sup>238</sup>Dixerat. Ille patris magni parere parabat <sup>239</sup>imperio; et primum pedibus talaria nectit <sup>240</sup>aurea, quae sublimem alis sive aequora supra <sup>241</sup>seu terram rapido pariter cum flamine portant; <sup>242</sup>tum virgam capit: hac animas ille evocat Orco <sup>243</sup>pallentis, alias sub Tartara tristia mittit, <sup>244</sup>dat somnos adimitque, et lumina morte resignat. <sup>245</sup>Illa fretus agit ventos, et turbida tranat <sup>246</sup>nubila; iamque volans apicem et latera ardua cernit <sup>247</sup>Atlantis duri, caelum qui vertice fulcit, <sup>248</sup>Atlantis, cinctum adsidue cui nubibus atris <sup>249</sup>piniferum caput et vento pulsatur et imbri; <sup>250</sup>nix umeros infusa tegit; tum flumina mento
translation.text: The loud report thro’ Libyan cities goes. Fame, the great ill, from small beginnings grows: Swift from the first; and ev’ry moment brings New vigour to her flights, new pinions to her wings. Soon grows the pigmy to gigantic size; Her feet on earth, her forehead in the skies. Inrag’d against the gods, revengeful Earth Produc’d her last of the Titanian birth. Swift is her walk, more swift her winged haste: A monstrous phantom, horrible and vast. As many plumes as raise her lofty flight, So many piercing eyes inlarge her sight; Millions of opening mouths to Fame belong, And ev’ry mouth is furnish’d with a tongue, And round with list’ning ears the flying plague is hung. She fills the peaceful universe with cries; No slumbers ever close her wakeful eyes; By day, from lofty tow’rs her head she shews, And spreads thro’ trembling crowds disastrous news; With court informers haunts, and royal spies; Things done relates, not done she feigns, and mingles truth with lies.

translation.original_text: <sup>251</sup>praecipitant senis, et glacie riget horrida barba. <sup>252</sup>Hic primum paribus nitens Cyllenius alis <sup>253</sup>constitit; hinc toto praeceps se corpore ad undas <sup>254</sup>misit, avi similis, quae circum litora, circum <sup>255</sup>piscosos scopulos humilis volat aequora iuxta. <sup>256</sup>Haud aliter terras inter caelumque volabat, <sup>257</sup>litus harenosum Libyae ventosque secabat <sup>258</sup>materno veniens ab avo Cyllenia proles. <sup>259</sup>Ut primum alatis tetigit magalia plantis, <sup>260</sup>Aenean fundantem arces ac tecta novantem <sup>261</sup>conspicit; atque illi stellatus iaspide fulva <sup>262</sup>ensis erat, Tyrioque ardebat murice laena <sup>263</sup>demissa ex umeris, dives quae munera Dido <sup>264</sup>fecerat, et tenui telas discreverat auro. <sup>265</sup>Continuo invadit: Tu nunc Karthaginis altae <sup>266</sup>fundamenta locas, pulchramque uxorius urbem <sup>267</sup>exstruis, heu regni rerumque oblite tuarum? <sup>268</sup>Ipse deum tibi me claro demittit Olympo <sup>269</sup>regnator, caelum ac terras qui numine torquet; <sup>270</sup>ipse haec ferre iubet celeris mandata per auras: <sup>271</sup>quid struis, aut qua spe Libycis teris otia terris? <sup>272</sup>Si te nulla movet tantarum gloria rerum, <sup>273</sup>nec super ipse tua moliris laude laborem, <sup>274</sup>Ascanium surgentem et spes heredis Iuli <sup>275</sup>respice, cui regnum Italiae Romanaque tellus
translation.text:
Talk is her business, and her chief delight To tell of prodigies and cause affright. She fills the people’s ears with Dido’s name, Who, lost to honour and the sense of shame, Admits into her throne and nuptial bed A wand’ring guest, who from his country fled: Whole days with him she passes in delights, And wastes in luxury long winter nights, Forgetful of her fame and royal trust, Dissolv’d in ease, abandon’d to her lust.

The goddess widely spreads the loud report, And flies at length to King Hyarba’s court. When first possess’d with this unwelcome news Whom did he not of men and gods accuse? This prince, from ravish’d Garamantis born, A hundred temples did with spoils adorn, In Ammon’s honour, his celestial sire; A hundred altars fed with wakeful fire; And, thro’ his vast dominions, priests ordain’d, Whose watchful care these holy rites maintain’d. The gates and columns were with garlands crown’d, And blood of victim beasts enrich’d the ground.

translation.original_text: <sup>276</sup>debentur. Tali Cyllenius ore locutus <sup>277</sup>mortalis visus medio sermone reliquit, <sup>278</sup>et procul in tenuem ex oculis evanuit auram. <sup>279</sup>At vero Aeneas aspectu obmutuit amens, <sup>280</sup>arrectaeque horrore comae, et vox faucibus haesit. <sup>281</sup>Ardet abire fuga dulcisque relinquere terras, <sup>282</sup>attonitus tanto monitu imperioque deorum. <sup>283</sup>Heu quid agat? Quo nunc reginam ambire furentem <sup>284</sup>audeat adfatu? Quae prima exordia sumat? <sup>285</sup>Atque animum nunc huc celerem, nunc dividit illuc, <sup>286</sup>in partisque rapit varias perque omnia versat. <sup>287</sup>Haec alternanti potior sententia visa est: <sup>288</sup>Mnesthea Sergestumque vocat fortemque Serestum, <sup>289</sup>classem aptent taciti sociosque ad litora cogant, <sup>290</sup>arma parent, et quae rebus sit causa novandis <sup>291</sup>dissimulent; sese interea, quando optuma Dido <sup>292</sup>nesciat et tantos rumpi non speret amores, <sup>293</sup>temptaturum aditus, et quae mollissima fandi <sup>294</sup>tempora, quis rebus dexter modus. Ocius omnes <sup>295</sup>imperio laeti parent ac iussa facessunt. <sup>296</sup>At regina dolos—quis fallere possit amantem? <sup>297</sup>praesensit, motusque excepit prima futuros, <sup>298</sup>omnia tuta timens. Eadem impia Fama furenti <sup>299</sup>detulit armari classem cursumque parari. <sup>300</sup>Saevit inops animi, totamque incensa per urbem
translation.text: He, when he heard a fugitive could move The Tyrian princess, who disdain’d his love, His breast with fury burn’d, his eyes with fire, Mad with despair, impatient with desire; Then on the sacred altars pouring wine, He thus with pray’rs implor’d his sire divine: “Great Jove! propitious to the Moorish race, Who feast on painted beds, with off’rings grace Thy temples, and adore thy pow’r divine With blood of victims, and with sparkling wine, Seest thou not this? or do we fear in vain Thy boasted thunder, and thy thoughtless reign? Do thy broad hands the forky lightnings lance? Thine are the bolts, or the blind work of chance? A wand’ring woman builds, within our state, A little town, bought at an easy rate; She pays me homage, and my grants allow A narrow space of Libyan lands to plow; Yet, scorning me, by passion blindly led, Admits a banish’d Trojan to her bed! And now this other Paris, with his train Of conquer’d cowards, must in Afric reign! (Whom, what they are, their looks and garb confess, Their locks with oil perfum’d, their Lydian dress.) He takes the spoil, enjoys the princely dame; And I, rejected I, adore an empty name.”

translation.original_text: <sup>301</sup>bacchatur, qualis commotis excita sacris <sup>302</sup>Thyias, ubi audito stimulant trieterica Baccho <sup>303</sup>orgia, nocturnusque vocat clamore Cithaeron. <sup>304</sup>Tandem his Aenean compellat vocibus ultro: <sup>305</sup>Dissimulare etiam sperasti, perfide, tantum <sup>306</sup>posse nefas, tacitusque mea decedere terra? <sup>307</sup>Nec te noster amor, nec te data dextera quondam, <sup>308</sup>nec moritura tenet crudeli funere Dido? <sup>309</sup>Quin etiam hiberno moliris sidere classem, <sup>310</sup>et mediis properas aquilonibus ire per altum, <sup>311</sup>crudelis? Quid, si non arva aliena domosque <sup>312</sup>ignotas peteres, sed Troia antiqua maneret, <sup>313</sup>Troia per undosum peteretur classibus aequor <sup>314</sup>Mene fugis? Per ego has lacrimas dextramque tuam te <sup>315</sup>(quando aliud mihi iam miserae nihil ipsa reliqui) <sup>316</sup>per conubia nostra, per inceptos hymenaeos, <sup>317</sup>si bene quid de te merui, fuit aut tibi quicquam <sup>318</sup>dulce meum, miserere domus labentis, et istam— <sup>319</sup>oro, si quis adhuc precibus locus—exue mentem. <sup>320</sup>Te propter Libycae gentes Nomadumque tyranni <sup>321</sup>odere, infensi Tyrii; te propter eundem <sup>322</sup>exstinctus pudor, et, qua sola sidera adibam, <sup>323</sup>fama prior. Cui me moribundam deseris, hospes? <sup>324</sup>Hoc solum nomen quoniam de coniuge restat. <sup>325</sup>Quid moror? An mea Pygmalion dum moenia frater
translation.text:
His vows, in haughty terms, he thus preferr’d, And held his altar’s horns. The mighty Thund’rer heard; Then cast his eyes on Carthage, where he found The lustful pair in lawless pleasure drown’d, Lost in their loves, insensible of shame, And both forgetful of their better fame. He calls Cyllenius, and the god attends, By whom his menacing command he sends: “Go, mount the western winds, and cleave the sky; Then, with a swift descent, to Carthage fly: There find the Trojan chief, who wastes his days In slothful riot and inglorious ease, Nor minds the future city, giv’n by fate. To him this message from my mouth relate: ‘Not so fair Venus hop’d, when twice she won Thy life with pray’rs, nor promis’d such a son. Hers was a hero, destin’d to command A martial race, and rule the Latian land, Who should his ancient line from Teucer draw, And on the conquer’d world impose the law.’ If glory cannot move a mind so mean, Nor future praise from fading pleasure wean, Yet why should he defraud his son of fame, And grudge the Romans their immortal name! What are his vain designs! what hopes he more From his long ling’ring on a hostile shore, Regardless to redeem his honour lost, And for his race to gain th’ Ausonian coast! Bid him with speed the Tyrian court forsake; With this command the slumb’ring warrior wake.”

Hermes obeys; with golden pinions binds His flying feet, and mounts the western winds: And, whether o’er the seas or earth he flies, With rapid force they bear him down the skies. But first he grasps within his awful hand The mark of sov’reign pow’r, his magic wand; With this he draws the ghosts from hollow graves; With this he drives them down the Stygian waves; With this he seals in sleep the wakeful sight, And eyes, tho’ clos’d in death, restores to light. Thus arm’d, the god begins his airy race, And drives the racking clouds along the liquid space; Now sees the tops of Atlas, as he flies, Whose brawny back supports the starry skies; Atlas, whose head, with piny forests crown’d, Is beaten by the winds, with foggy vapours bound. Snows hide his shoulders; from beneath his chin The founts of rolling streams their race begin; A beard of ice on his large breast depends. Here, pois’d upon his wings, the god descends: Then, rested thus, he from the tow’ring height Plung’d downward, with precipitated flight, Lights on the seas, and skims along the flood. As waterfowl, who seek their fishy food, Less, and yet less, to distant prospect show; By turns they dance aloft, and dive below: Like these, the steerage of his wings he plies, And near the surface of the water flies, Till, having pass’d the seas, and cross’d the sands, He clos’d his wings, and stoop’d on Libyan lands: Where shepherds once were hous’d in homely sheds, Now tow’rs within the clouds advance their heads. Arriving there, he found the Trojan prince New ramparts raising for the town’s defence. A purple scarf, with gold embroider’d o’er, (Queen Dido’s gift,) about his waist he wore; A sword, with glitt’ring gems diversified, For ornament, not use, hung idly by his side.

translation.original_text: <sup>326</sup>destruat, aut captam ducat Gaetulus Iarbas? <sup>327</sup>Saltem si qua mihi de te suscepta fuisset <sup>328</sup>ante fugam suboles, si quis mihi parvulus aula <sup>329</sup>luderet Aeneas, qui te tamen ore referret, <sup>330</sup>non equidem omnino capta ac deserta viderer. <sup>331</sup>Dixerat. Ille Iovis monitis immota tenebat <sup>332</sup>lumina, et obnixus curam sub corde premebat. <sup>333</sup>Tandem pauca refert: Ego te, quae plurima fando <sup>334</sup>enumerare vales, numquam, regina, negabo <sup>335</sup>promeritam; nec me meminisse pigebit Elissae, <sup>336</sup>dum memor ipse mei, dum spiritus hos regit artus. <sup>337</sup>Pro re pauca loquar. Neque ego hanc abscondere furto <sup>338</sup>speravi—ne finge—fugam, nec coniugis umquam <sup>339</sup>praetendi taedas, aut haec in foedera veni. <sup>340</sup>Me si fata meis paterentur ducere vitam <sup>341</sup>auspiciis et sponte mea componere curas, <sup>342</sup>urbem Troianam primum dulcisque meorum <sup>343</sup>reliquias colerem, Priami tecta alta manerent, <sup>344</sup>et recidiva manu posuissem Pergama victis. <sup>345</sup>Sed nunc Italiam magnam Gryneus Apollo, <sup>346</sup>Italiam Lyciae iussere capessere sortes: <sup>347</sup>hic amor, haec patria est. Si te Karthaginis arces, <sup>348</sup>Phoenissam, Libycaeque aspectus detinet urbis, <sup>349</sup>quae tandem, Ausonia Teucros considere terra, <sup>350</sup>invidia est? Et nos fas extera quaerere regna.
translation.text: Then thus, with winged words, the god began, Resuming his own shape: “Degenerate man, Thou woman’s property, what mak’st thou here, These foreign walls and Tyrian tow’rs to rear, Forgetful of thy own? All-pow’rful Jove, Who sways the world below and heav’n above, Has sent me down with this severe command: What means thy ling’ring in the Libyan land? If glory cannot move a mind so mean, Nor future praise from flitting pleasure wean, Regard the fortunes of thy rising heir: The promis’d crown let young Ascanius wear, To whom th’ Ausonian scepter, and the state Of Rome’s imperial name is ow’d by fate.” So spoke the god; and, speaking, took his flight, Involv’d in clouds, and vanish’d out of sight.

translation.original_text: <sup>351</sup>Me patris Anchisae, quotiens umentibus umbris <sup>352</sup>nox operit terras, quotiens astra ignea surgunt, <sup>353</sup>admonet in somnis et turbida terret imago; <sup>354</sup>me puer Ascanius capitisque iniuria cari, <sup>355</sup>quem regno Hesperiae fraudo et fatalibus arvis. <sup>356</sup>Nunc etiam interpres divom, Iove missus ab ipso— <sup>357</sup>testor utrumque caput—celeris mandata per auras <sup>358</sup>detulit; ipse deum manifesto in lumine vidi <sup>359</sup>intrantem muros, vocemque his auribus hausi. <sup>360</sup>Desine meque tuis incendere teque querelis: <sup>361</sup>Italiam non sponte sequor. <sup>362</sup>Talia dicentem iamdudum aversa tuetur, <sup>363</sup>huc illuc volvens oculos, totumque pererrat <sup>364</sup>luminibus tacitis, et sic accensa profatur: <sup>365</sup>Nec tibi diva parens, generis nec Dardanus auctor, <sup>366</sup>perfide; sed duris genuit te cautibus horrens <sup>367</sup>Caucasus, Hyrcanaeque admorunt ubera tigres. <sup>368</sup>Nam quid dissimulo, aut quae me ad maiora reservo? <sup>369</sup>Num fletu ingemuit nostro? Num lumina flexit? <sup>370</sup>Num lacrimas victus dedit, aut miseratus amantem est? <sup>371</sup>Quae quibus anteferam? Iam iam nec maxuma Iuno, <sup>372</sup>nec Saturnius haec oculis pater aspicit aequis. <sup>373</sup>Nusquam tuta fides. Eiectum litore, egentem <sup>374</sup>excepi, et regni demens in parte locavi; <sup>375</sup>amissam classem, socios a morte reduxi.
translation.text:
The pious prince was seiz’d with sudden fear; Mute was his tongue, and upright stood his hair. Revolving in his mind the stern command, He longs to fly, and loathes the charming land. What should he say? or how should he begin? What course, alas! remains to steer between Th’ offended lover and the pow’rful queen? This way and that he turns his anxious mind, And all expedients tries, and none can find. Fix’d on the deed, but doubtful of the means, After long thought, to this advice he leans: Three chiefs he calls, commands them to repair The fleet, and ship their men with silent care; Some plausible pretence he bids them find, To colour what in secret he design’d. Himself, meantime, the softest hours would choose, Before the love-sick lady heard the news; And move her tender mind, by slow degrees, To suffer what the sov’reign pow’r decrees: Jove will inspire him, when, and what to say. They hear with pleasure, and with haste obey.

But soon the queen perceives the thin disguise: (What arts can blind a jealous woman’s eyes!) She was the first to find the secret fraud, Before the fatal news was blaz’d abroad. Love the first motions of the lover hears, Quick to presage, and ev’n in safety fears. Nor impious Fame was wanting to report The ships repair’d, the Trojans’ thick resort, And purpose to forsake the Tyrian court. Frantic with fear, impatient of the wound, And impotent of mind, she roves the city round. Less wild the Bacchanalian dames appear, When, from afar, their nightly god they hear, And howl about the hills, and shake the wreathy spear. At length she finds the dear perfidious man; Prevents his form’d excuse, and thus began: “Base and ungrateful! could you hope to fly, And undiscover’d scape a lover’s eye? Nor could my kindness your compassion move. Nor plighted vows, nor dearer bands of love? Or is the death of a despairing queen Not worth preventing, tho’ too well foreseen? Ev’n when the wintry winds command your stay, You dare the tempests, and defy the sea. False as you are, suppose you were not bound To lands unknown, and foreign coasts to sound; Were Troy restor’d, and Priam’s happy reign, Now durst you tempt, for Troy, the raging main? See whom you fly! am I the foe you shun? Now, by those holy vows, so late begun, By this right hand, (since I have nothing more To challenge, but the faith you gave before;) I beg you by these tears too truly shed, By the new pleasures of our nuptial bed; If ever Dido, when you most were kind, Were pleasing in your eyes, or touch’d your mind; By these my pray’rs, if pray’rs may yet have place, Pity the fortunes of a falling race. For you I have provok’d a tyrant’s hate, Incens’d the Libyan and the Tyrian state; For you alone I suffer in my fame, Bereft of honour, and expos’d to shame. Whom have I now to trust, ungrateful guest? (That only name remains of all the rest!) What have I left? or whither can I fly? Must I attend Pygmalion’s cruelty, Or till Hyarba shall in triumph lead A queen that proudly scorn’d his proffer’d bed? Had you deferr’d, at least, your hasty flight, And left behind some pledge of our delight, Some babe to bless the mother’s mournful sight, Some young Aeneas, to supply your place, Whose features might express his father’s face; I should not then complain to live bereft Of all my husband, or be wholly left.”

translation.original_text: <sup>376</sup>Heu furiis incensa feror! Nunc augur Apollo, <sup>377</sup>nunc Lyciae sortes, nunc et Iove missus ab ipso <sup>378</sup>interpres divom fert horrida iussa per auras. <sup>379</sup>Scilicet is Superis labor est, ea cura quietos <sup>380</sup>sollicitat. Neque te teneo, neque dicta refello. <sup>381</sup>I, sequere Italiam ventis, pete regna per undas. <sup>382</sup>Spero equidem mediis, si quid pia numina possunt, <sup>383</sup>supplicia hausurum scopulis, et nomine Dido <sup>384</sup>saepe vocaturum. Sequar atris ignibus absens, <sup>385</sup>et, cum frigida mors anima seduxerit artus, <sup>386</sup>omnibus umbra locis adero. Dabis, improbe, poenas. <sup>387</sup>Audiam et haec Manis veniet mihi fama sub imos. <sup>388</sup>His medium dictis sermonem abrumpit, et auras <sup>389</sup>aegra fugit, seque ex oculis avertit et aufert, <sup>390</sup>linquens multa metu cunctantem et multa parantem <sup>391</sup>dicere. Suscipiunt famulae, conlapsaque membra <sup>392</sup>marmoreo referunt thalamo stratisque reponunt. <sup>393</sup>At pius Aeneas, quamquam lenire dolentem <sup>394</sup>solando cupit et dictis avertere curas, <sup>395</sup>multa gemens magnoque animum labefactus amore, <sup>396</sup>iussa tamen divom exsequitur, classemque revisit. <sup>397</sup>Tum vero Teucri incumbunt, et litore celsas <sup>398</sup>deducunt toto naves: natat uncta carina; <sup>399</sup>frondentisque ferunt remos et robora silvis <sup>400</sup>infabricata, fugae studio.
translation.text: Here paus’d the queen. Unmov’d he holds his eyes, By Jove’s command; nor suffer’d love to rise, Tho’ heaving in his heart; and thus at length replies: “Fair queen, you never can enough repeat Your boundless favours, or I own my debt; Nor can my mind forget Eliza’s name, While vital breath inspires this mortal frame. This only let me speak in my defence: I never hop’d a secret flight from hence, Much less pretended to the lawful claim Of sacred nuptials, or a husband’s name. For, if indulgent Heav’n would leave me free, And not submit my life to fate’s decree, My choice would lead me to the Trojan shore, Those relics to review, their dust adore, And Priam’s ruin’d palace to restore. But now the Delphian oracle commands, And fate invites me to the Latian lands. That is the promis’d place to which I steer, And all my vows are terminated there. If you, a Tyrian, and a stranger born, With walls and tow’rs a Libyan town adorn, Why may not we, like you, a foreign race, Like you, seek shelter in a foreign place? As often as the night obscures the skies With humid shades, or twinkling stars arise, Anchises’ angry ghost in dreams appears, Chides my delay, and fills my soul with fears; And young Ascanius justly may complain Of his defrauded and destin’d reign. Ev’n now the herald of the gods appear’d: Waking I saw him, and his message heard. From Jove he came commission’d, heav’nly bright With radiant beams, and manifest to sight (The sender and the sent I both attest) These walls he enter’d, and those words express’d. Fair queen, oppose not what the gods command; Forc’d by my fate, I leave your happy land.”

translation.original_text: <sup>401</sup>Migrantis cernas, totaque ex urbe ruentis. <sup>402</sup>Ac velut ingentem formicae farris acervum <sup>403</sup>cum populant, hiemis memores, tectoque reponunt; <sup>404</sup>it nigrum campis agmen, praedamque per herbas <sup>405</sup>convectant calle angusto; pars grandia trudunt <sup>406</sup>obnixae frumenta umeris; pars agmina cogunt <sup>407</sup>castigantque moras; opere omnis semita fervet. <sup>408</sup>Quis tibi tum, Dido, cernenti talia sensus? <sup>409</sup>quosve dabas gemitus, cum litora fervere late <sup>410</sup>prospiceres arce ex summa, totumque videres <sup>411</sup>misceri ante oculos tantis clamoribus aequor? <sup>412</sup>Improbe Amor, quid non mortalia pectora cogis? <sup>413</sup>Ire iterum in lacrimas, iterum temptare precando <sup>414</sup>cogitur, et supplex animos submittere amori, <sup>415</sup>ne quid inexpertum frustra moritura relinquat. <sup>416</sup>Anna, vides toto properari litore; circum <sup>417</sup>undique convenere; vocat iam carbasus auras, <sup>418</sup>puppibus et laeti nautae imposuere coronas. <sup>419</sup>Hunc ego si potui tantum sperare dolorem, <sup>420</sup>et perferre, soror, potero. Miserae hoc tamen unum <sup>421</sup>exsequere, Anna, mihi. Solam nam perfidus ille <sup>422</sup>te colere, arcanos etiam tibi credere sensus; <sup>423</sup>sola viri mollis aditus et tempora noras. <sup>424</sup>I, soror, atque hostem supplex adfare superbum: <sup>425</sup>non ego cum Danais Troianam exscindere gentem
translation.text:
Thus while he spoke, already she began, With sparkling eyes, to view the guilty man; From head to foot survey’d his person o’er, Nor longer these outrageous threats forebore: “False as thou art, and, more than false, forsworn! Not sprung from noble blood, nor goddess-born, But hewn from harden’d entrails of a rock! And rough Hyrcanian tigers gave thee suck! Why should I fawn? what have I worse to fear? Did he once look, or lent a list’ning ear, Sigh’d when I sobb’d, or shed one kindly tear? All symptoms of a base ungrateful mind, So foul, that, which is worse, ’tis hard to find. Of man’s injustice why should I complain? The gods, and Jove himself, behold in vain Triumphant treason; yet no thunder flies, Nor Juno views my wrongs with equal eyes; Faithless is earth, and faithless are the skies! Justice is fled, and Truth is now no more! I sav’d the shipwreck’d exile on my shore; With needful food his hungry Trojans fed; I took the traitor to my throne and bed: Fool that I was—— ’tis little to repeat The rest, I stor’d and rigg’d his ruin’d fleet. I rave, I rave! A god’s command he pleads, And makes Heav’n accessary to his deeds. Now Lycian lots, and now the Delian god, Now Hermes is employ’d from Jove’s abode, To warn him hence; as if the peaceful state Of heav’nly pow’rs were touch’d with human fate! But go! thy flight no longer I detain; Go seek thy promis’d kingdom thro’ the main! Yet, if the heav’ns will hear my pious vow, The faithless waves, not half so false as thou, Or secret sands, shall sepulchers afford To thy proud vessels, and their perjur’d lord. Then shalt thou call on injur’d Dido’s name: Dido shall come in a black sulph’ry flame, When death has once dissolv’d her mortal frame; Shall smile to see the traitor vainly weep: Her angry ghost, arising from the deep, Shall haunt thee waking, and disturb thy sleep. At least my shade thy punishment shall know, And Fame shall spread the pleasing news below.”

Abruptly here she stops; then turns away Her loathing eyes, and shuns the sight of day. Amaz’d he stood, revolving in his mind What speech to frame, and what excuse to find. Her fearful maids their fainting mistress led, And softly laid her on her ivory bed.

translation.original_text: <sup>426</sup>Aulide iuravi, classemve ad Pergama misi, <sup>427</sup>nec patris Anchisae cineres Manisve revelli, <sup>428</sup>cur mea dicta neget duras demittere in auris. <sup>429</sup>Quo ruit? Extremum hoc miserae det munus amanti: <sup>430</sup>exspectet facilemque fugam ventosque ferentis. <sup>431</sup>Non iam coniugium antiquum, quod prodidit, oro, <sup>432</sup>nec pulchro ut Latio careat regnumque relinquat: <sup>433</sup>tempus inane peto, requiem spatiumque furori, <sup>434</sup>dum mea me victam doceat fortuna dolere. <sup>435</sup>Extremam hanc oro veniam—miserere sororis— <sup>436</sup>quam mihi cum dederit, cumulatam morte remittam. <sup>437</sup>Talibus orabat, talisque miserrima fletus <sup>438</sup>fertque refertque soror: sed nullis ille movetur <sup>439</sup>fletibus, aut voces ullas tractabilis audit; <sup>440</sup>fata obstant, placidasque viri deus obstruit auris. <sup>441</sup>Ac, velut annoso validam cum robore quercum <sup>442</sup>Alpini Boreae nunc hinc nunc flatibus illinc <sup>443</sup>eruere inter se certant; it stridor, et altae <sup>444</sup>consternunt terram concusso stipite frondes; <sup>445</sup>ipsa haeret scopulis, et, quantum vertice ad auras <sup>446</sup>aetherias, tantum radice in Tartara tendit: <sup>447</sup>haud secus adsiduis hinc atque hinc vocibus heros <sup>448</sup>tunditur, et magno persentit pectore curas; <sup>449</sup>mens immota manet; lacrimae volvuntur inanes. <sup>450</sup>Tum vero infelix fatis exterrita Dido
translation.text:
But good Aeneas, tho’ he much desir’d To give that pity which her grief requir’d; Tho’ much he mourn’d, and labour’d with his love, Resolv’d at length, obeys the will of Jove; Reviews his forces: they with early care Unmoor their vessels, and for sea prepare. The fleet is soon afloat, in all its pride, And well-calk’d galleys in the harbour ride. Then oaks for oars they fell’d; or, as they stood, Of its green arms despoil’d the growing wood, Studious of flight. The beach is cover’d o’er With Trojan bands, that blacken all the shore: On ev’ry side are seen, descending down, Thick swarms of soldiers, loaden from the town. Thus, in battalia, march embodied ants, Fearful of winter, and of future wants, T’ invade the corn, and to their cells convey The plunder’d forage of their yellow prey. The sable troops, along the narrow tracks, Scarce bear the weighty burthen on their backs: Some set their shoulders to the pond’rous grain; Some guard the spoil; some lash the lagging train; All ply their sev’ral tasks, and equal toil sustain.

What pangs the tender breast of Dido tore, When, from the tow’r, she saw the cover’d shore, And heard the shouts of sailors from afar, Mix’d with the murmurs of the wat’ry war! All-pow’rful Love! what changes canst thou cause In human hearts, subjected to thy laws! Once more her haughty soul the tyrant bends: To pray’rs and mean submissions she descends. No female arts or aids she left untried, Nor counsels unexplor’d, before she died. “Look, Anna! look! the Trojans crowd to sea; They spread their canvas, and their anchors weigh. The shouting crew their ships with garlands bind, Invoke the sea gods, and invite the wind. Could I have thought this threat’ning blow so near, My tender soul had been forewarn’d to bear. But do not you my last request deny; With yon perfidious man your int’rest try, And bring me news, if I must live or die. You are his fav’rite; you alone can find The dark recesses of his inmost mind: In all his trusted secrets you have part, And know the soft approaches to his heart. Haste then, and humbly seek my haughty foe; Tell him, I did not with the Grecians go, Nor did my fleet against his friends employ, Nor swore the ruin of unhappy Troy, Nor mov’d with hands profane his father’s dust: Why should he then reject a suit so just! Whom does he shun, and whither would he fly! Can he this last, this only pray’r deny! Let him at least his dang’rous flight delay, Wait better winds, and hope a calmer sea. The nuptials he disclaims I urge no more: Let him pursue the promis’d Latian shore. A short delay is all I ask him now; A pause of grief, an interval from woe, Till my soft soul be temper’d to sustain Accustom’d sorrows, and inur’d to pain. If you in pity grant this one request, My death shall glut the hatred of his breast.” This mournful message pious Anna bears, And seconds with her own her sister’s tears: But all her arts are still employ’d in vain; Again she comes, and is refus’d again. His harden’d heart nor pray’rs nor threat’nings move; Fate, and the god, had stopp’d his ears to love.

translation.original_text: <sup>451</sup>mortem orat; taedet caeli convexa tueri. <sup>452</sup>Quo magis inceptum peragat lucemque relinquat, <sup>453</sup>vidit, turicremis cum dona imponeret aris, <sup>454</sup>horrendum dictu, latices nigrescere sacros, <sup>455</sup>fusaque in obscenum se vertere vina cruorem. <sup>456</sup>Hoc visum nulli, non ipsi effata sorori. <sup>457</sup>Praeterea fuit in tectis de marmore templum <sup>458</sup>coniugis antiqui, miro quod honore colebat, <sup>459</sup>velleribus niveis et festa fronde revinctum: <sup>460</sup>hinc exaudiri voces et verba vocantis <sup>461</sup>visa viri, nox cum terras obscura teneret; <sup>462</sup>solaque culminibus ferali carmine bubo <sup>463</sup>saepe queri et longas in fletum ducere voces; <sup>464</sup>multaque praeterea vatum praedicta priorum <sup>465</sup>terribili monitu horrificant. Agit ipse furentem <sup>466</sup>in somnis ferus Aeneas; semperque relinqui <sup>467</sup>sola sibi, semper longam incomitata videtur <sup>468</sup>ire viam, et Tyrios deserta quaerere terra. <sup>469</sup>Eumenidum veluti demens videt agmina Pentheus, <sup>470</sup>et solem geminum et duplicis se ostendere Thebas; <sup>471</sup>aut Agamemnonius scaenis agitatus Orestes <sup>472</sup>armatam facibus matrem et serpentibus atris <sup>473</sup>cum fugit, ultricesque sedent in limine Dirae. <sup>474</sup>Ergo ubi concepit furias evicta dolore <sup>475</sup>decrevitque mori, tempus secum ipsa modumque
translation.text: As, when the winds their airy quarrel try, Justling from ev’ry quarter of the sky, This way and that the mountain oak they bend, His boughs they shatter, and his branches rend; With leaves and falling mast they spread the ground; The hollow valleys echo to the sound: Unmov’d, the royal plant their fury mocks, Or, shaken, clings more closely to the rocks; Far as he shoots his tow’ring head on high, So deep in earth his fix’d foundations lie. No less a storm the Trojan hero bears; Thick messages and loud complaints he hears, And bandied words, still beating on his ears. Sighs, groans, and tears proclaim his inward pains; But the firm purpose of his heart remains.

translation.original_text: <sup>476</sup>exigit, et, maestam dictis adgressa sororem, <sup>477</sup>consilium voltu tegit, ac spem fronte serenat: <sup>478</sup>Inveni, germana, viam—gratare sorori— <sup>479</sup>quae mihi reddat eum, vel eo me solvat amantem. <sup>480</sup>Oceani finem iuxta solemque cadentem <sup>481</sup>ultimus Aethiopum locus est, ubi maxumus Atlas <sup>482</sup>axem humero torquet stellis ardentibus aptum: <sup>483</sup>hinc mihi Massylae gentis monstrata sacerdos, <sup>484</sup>Hesperidum templi custos, epulasque draconi <sup>485</sup>quae dabat, et sacros servabat in arbore ramos, <sup>486</sup>spargens umida mella soporiferumque papaver. <sup>487</sup>Haec se carminibus promittit solvere mentes <sup>488</sup>quas velit, ast aliis duras immittere curas, <sup>489</sup>sistere aquam fluviis, et vertere sidera retro; <sup>490</sup>nocturnosque movet Manis: mugire videbis <sup>491</sup>sub pedibus terram, et descendere montibus ornos. <sup>492</sup>Testor, cara, deos et te, germana, tuumque <sup>493</sup>dulce caput, magicas invitam accingier artes. <sup>494</sup>Tu secreta pyram tecto interiore sub auras <sup>495</sup>erige, et arma viri, thalamo quae fixa reliquit <sup>496</sup>impius, exuviasque omnis, lectumque iugalem, <sup>497</sup>quo perii, superimponas: abolere nefandi <sup>498</sup>cuncta viri monumenta iuvat, monstratque sacerdos. <sup>499</sup>Haec effata silet; pallor simul occupat ora. <sup>500</sup>Non tamen Anna novis praetexere funera sacris
translation.text:
The wretched queen, pursued by cruel fate, Begins at length the light of heav’n to hate, And loathes to live. Then dire portents she sees, To hasten on the death her soul decrees: Strange to relate! for when, before the shrine, She pours in sacrifice the purple wine, The purple wine is turn’d to putrid blood, And the white offer’d milk converts to mud. This dire presage, to her alone reveal’d, From all, and ev’n her sister, she conceal’d. A marble temple stood within the grove, Sacred to death, and to her murder’d love; That honour’d chapel she had hung around With snowy fleeces, and with garlands crown’d: Oft, when she visited this lonely dome, Strange voices issued from her husband’s tomb; She thought she heard him summon her away, Invite her to his grave, and chide her stay. Hourly ’tis heard, when with a boding note The solitary screech owl strains her throat, And, on a chimney’s top, or turret’s height, With songs obscene disturbs the silence of the night. Besides, old prophecies augment her fears; And stern Aeneas in her dreams appears, Disdainful as by day: she seems, alone, To wander in her sleep, thro’ ways unknown, Guideless and dark; or, in a desert plain, To seek her subjects, and to seek in vain: Like Pentheus, when, distracted with his fear, He saw two suns, and double Thebes, appear; Or mad Orestes, when his mother’s ghost Full in his face infernal torches toss’d, And shook her snaky locks: he shuns the sight, Flies o’er the stage, surpris’d with mortal fright; The Furies guard the door and intercept his flight.

Now, sinking underneath a load of grief, From death alone she seeks her last relief; The time and means resolv’d within her breast, She to her mournful sister thus address’d (Dissembling hope, her cloudy front she clears, And a false vigour in her eyes appears): “Rejoice!” she said. “Instructed from above, My lover I shall gain, or lose my love. Nigh rising Atlas, next the falling sun, Long tracts of Ethiopian climates run: There a Massylian priestess I have found, Honour’d for age, for magic arts renown’d: Th’ Hesperian temple was her trusted care; ’Twas she supplied the wakeful dragon’s fare. She poppy seeds in honey taught to steep, Reclaim’d his rage, and sooth’d him into sleep. She watch’d the golden fruit; her charms unbind The chains of love, or fix them on the mind: She stops the torrents, leaves the channel dry, Repels the stars, and backward bears the sky. The yawning earth rebellows to her call, Pale ghosts ascend, and mountain ashes fall. Witness, ye gods, and thou my better part, How loth I am to try this impious art! Within the secret court, with silent care, Erect a lofty pile, expos’d in air: Hang on the topmost part the Trojan vest, Spoils, arms, and presents, of my faithless guest. Next, under these, the bridal bed be plac’d, Where I my ruin in his arms embrac’d: All relics of the wretch are doom’d to fire; For so the priestess and her charms require.”

translation.original_text: <sup>501</sup>germanam credit, nec tantos mente furores <sup>502</sup>concipit, aut graviora timet, quam morte Sychaei: <sup>503</sup>ergo iussa parat. <sup>504</sup>At regina, pyra penetrali in sede sub auras <sup>505</sup>erecta ingenti taedis atque ilice secta, <sup>506</sup>intenditque locum sertis, et fronde coronat <sup>507</sup>funerea; super exuvias ensemque relictum <sup>508</sup>effigiemque toro locat, haud ignara futuri. <sup>509</sup>Stant arae circum, et crines effusa sacerdos <sup>510</sup>ter centum tonat ore deos, Erebumque Chaosque, <sup>511</sup>tergeminamque Hecaten, tria virginis ora Dianae. <sup>512</sup>Sparserat et latices simulatos fontis Averni, <sup>513</sup>falcibus et messae ad lunam quaeruntur aënis <sup>514</sup>pubentes herbae nigri cum lacte veneni; <sup>515</sup>quaeritur et nascentis equi de fronte revolsus <sup>516</sup>et matri praereptus amor. <sup>517</sup>Ipsa mola manibusque piis altaria iuxta, <sup>518</sup>unum exuta pedem vinclis, in veste recincta, <sup>519</sup>testatur moritura deos et conscia fati <sup>520</sup>sidera; tum, si quod non aequo foedere amantes <sup>521</sup>curae numen habet iustumque memorque, precatur. <sup>522</sup>Nox erat, et placidum carpebant fessa soporem <sup>523</sup>corpora per terras, silvaeque et saeva quierant <sup>524</sup>aequora: cum medio volvuntur sidera lapsu, <sup>525</sup>cum tacet omnis ager, pecudes pictaeque volucres,
translation.text: Thus far she said, and farther speech forbears; A mortal paleness in her face appears: Yet the mistrustless Anna could not find The secret fun’ral in these rites design’d; Nor thought so dire a rage possess’d her mind. Unknowing of a train conceal’d so well, She fear’d no worse than when Sichaeus fell; Therefore obeys. The fatal pile they rear, Within the secret court, expos’d in air. The cloven holms and pines are heap’d on high, And garlands on the hollow spaces lie. Sad cypress, vervain, yew, compose the wreath, And ev’ry baleful green denoting death. The queen, determin’d to the fatal deed, The spoils and sword he left, in order spread, And the man’s image on the nuptial bed.

translation.original_text: <sup>526</sup>quaeque lacus late liquidos, quaeque aspera dumis <sup>527</sup>rura tenent, somno positae sub nocte silenti <sup>528</sup>lenibant curas, et corda oblita laborum. <sup>529</sup>At non infelix animi Phoenissa, nec umquam <sup>530</sup>Solvitur in somnos, oculisve aut pectore noctem <sup>531</sup>accipit: ingeminant curae, rursusque resurgens <sup>532</sup>saevit amor, magnoque irarum fluctuat aestu. <sup>533</sup>Sic adeo insistit, secumque ita corde volutat: <sup>534</sup>En, quid ago? Rursusne procos inrisa priores <sup>535</sup>experiar, Nomadumque petam conubia supplex, <sup>536</sup>quos ego sim totiens iam dedignata maritos? <sup>537</sup>Iliacas igitur classes atque ultima Teucrum <sup>538</sup>iussa sequar? Quiane auxilio iuvat ante levatos, <sup>539</sup>et bene apud memores veteris stat gratia facti? <sup>540</sup>Quis me autem, fac velle, sinet, ratibusve superbis <sup>541</sup>invisam accipiet? Nescis heu, perdita, necdum <sup>542</sup>Laomedonteae sentis periuria gentis? <sup>543</sup>Quid tum, sola fuga nautas comitabor ovantes, <sup>544</sup>an Tyriis omnique manu stipata meorum <sup>545</sup>inferar, et, quos Sidonia vix urbe revelli, <sup>546</sup>rursus agam pelago, et ventis dare vela iubebo? <sup>547</sup>Quin morere, ut merita es, ferroque averte dolorem. <sup>548</sup>Tu lacrimis evicta meis, tu prima furentem <sup>549</sup>his, germana, malis oneras atque obicis hosti. <sup>550</sup>Non licuit thalami expertem sine crimine vitam
translation.text:
And now (the sacred altars plac’d around) The priestess enters, with her hair unbound, And thrice invokes the pow’rs below the ground. Night, Erebus, and Chaos she proclaims, And threefold Hecate, with her hundred names, And three Dianas: next, she sprinkles round With feign’d Avernian drops the hallow’d ground; Culls hoary simples, found by Phoebe’s light, With brazen sickles reap’d at noon of night; Then mixes baleful juices in the bowl, And cuts the forehead of a newborn foal, Robbing the mother’s love. The destin’d queen Observes, assisting at the rites obscene; A leaven’d cake in her devoted hands She holds, and next the highest altar stands: One tender foot was shod, her other bare; Girt was her gather’d gown, and loose her hair. Thus dress’d, she summon’d, with her dying breath, The heav’ns and planets conscious of her death, And ev’ry pow’r, if any rules above, Who minds, or who revenges, injur’d love.

“’Twas dead of night, when weary bodies close Their eyes in balmy sleep and soft repose: The winds no longer whisper thro’ the woods, Nor murm’ring tides disturb the gentle floods. The stars in silent order mov’d around; And Peace, with downy wings, was brooding on the ground The flocks and herds, and party-colour’d fowl, Which haunt the woods, or swim the weedy pool, Stretch’d on the quiet earth, securely lay, Forgetting the past labours of the day. All else of nature’s common gift partake: Unhappy Dido was alone awake. Nor sleep nor ease the furious queen can find; Sleep fled her eyes, as quiet fled her mind. Despair, and rage, and love divide her heart; Despair and rage had some, but love the greater part.

translation.original_text: <sup>551</sup>degere, more ferae, tales nec tangere curas! <sup>552</sup>Non servata fides cineri promissa Sychaeo! <sup>553</sup>Tantos illa suo rumpebat pectore questus. <sup>554</sup>Aeneas celsa in puppi, iam certus eundi, <sup>555</sup>carpebat somnos, rebus iam rite paratis. <sup>556</sup>Huic se forma dei voltu redeuntis eodem <sup>557</sup>obtulit in somnis, rursusque ita visa monere est— <sup>558</sup>omnia Mercurio similis, vocemque coloremque <sup>559</sup>et crinis flavos et membra decora iuventa: <sup>560</sup>Nate dea, potes hoc sub casu ducere somnos, <sup>561</sup>nec, quae te circum stent deinde pericula, cernis, <sup>562</sup>demens, nec Zephyros audis spirare secundos? <sup>563</sup>Illa dolos dirumque nefas in pectore versat, <sup>564</sup>certa mori, varioque irarum fluctuat aestu. <sup>565</sup>Non fugis hinc praeceps, dum praecipitare potestas? <sup>566</sup>Iam mare turbari trabibus, saevasque videbis <sup>567</sup>conlucere faces, iam fervere litora flammis, <sup>568</sup>si te his attigerit terris Aurora morantem. <sup>569</sup>Heia age, rumpe moras. Varium et mutabile semper <sup>570</sup>femina. Sic fatus, nocti se immiscuit atrae. <sup>571</sup>Tum vero Aeneas, subitis exterritus umbris, <sup>572</sup>corripit e somno corpus, sociosque fatigat: <sup>573</sup>Praecipites vigilate, viri, et considite transtris; <sup>574</sup>solvite vela citi. Deus aethere missus ab alto <sup>575</sup>festinare fugam tortosque incidere funes
translation.text: Then thus she said within her secret mind: “What shall I do? what succour can I find? Become a suppliant to Hyarba’s pride, And take my turn, to court and be denied? Shall I with this ungrateful Trojan go, Forsake an empire, and attend a foe? Himself I refug’d, and his train reliev’d; ’Tis true; but am I sure to be receiv’d? Can gratitude in Trojan souls have place! Laomedon still lives in all his race! Then, shall I seek alone the churlish crew, Or with my fleet their flying sails pursue? What force have I but those whom scarce before I drew reluctant from their native shore? Will they again embark at my desire, Once more sustain the seas, and quit their second Tyre? Rather with steel thy guilty breast invade, And take the fortune thou thyself hast made. Your pity, sister, first seduc’d my mind, Or seconded too well what I design’d. These dear-bought pleasures had I never known, Had I continued free, and still my own; Avoiding love, I had not found despair, But shar’d with salvage beasts the common air. Like them, a lonely life I might have led, Not mourn’d the living, nor disturb’d the dead.” These thoughts she brooded in her anxious breast. On board, the Trojan found more easy rest. Resolv’d to sail, in sleep he pass’d the night; And order’d all things for his early flight.

translation.original_text: <sup>576</sup>ecce iterum stimulat. Sequimur te, sancte deorum, <sup>577</sup>quisquis es, imperioque iterum paremus ovantes. <sup>578</sup>Adsis o placidusque iuves, et sidera caelo <sup>579</sup>dextra feras. Dixit, vaginaque eripit ensem <sup>580</sup>fulmineum, strictoque ferit retinacula ferro. <sup>581</sup>Idem omnes simul ardor habet, rapiuntque ruuntque; <sup>582</sup>litora deseruere; latet sub classibus aequor; <sup>583</sup>adnixi torquent spumas et caerula verrunt. <sup>584</sup>Et iam prima novo spargebat lumine terras <sup>585</sup>Tithoni croceum linquens Aurora cubile. <sup>586</sup>Regina e speculis ut primum albescere lucem <sup>587</sup>vidit, et aequatis classem procedere velis, <sup>588</sup>litoraque et vacuos sensit sine remige portus, <sup>589</sup>terque quaterque manu pectus percussa decorum, <sup>590</sup>flaventesque abscissa comas, Pro Iuppiter, ibit <sup>591</sup>hic ait et nostris inluserit advena regnis? <sup>592</sup>Non arma expedient, totaque ex urbe sequentur, <sup>593</sup>deripientque rates alii navalibus? Ite, <sup>594</sup>ferte citi flammas, date vela, impellite remos!— <sup>595</sup>Quid loquor, aut ubi sum? Quae mentem insania mutat? <sup>596</sup>Infelix Dido, nunc te facta impia tangunt. <sup>597</sup>Tum decuit, cum sceptra dabas.—En dextra fidesque, <sup>598</sup>quem secum patrios aiunt portare Penates, <sup>599</sup>quem subiisse umeris confectum aetate parentem! <sup>600</sup>Non potui abreptum divellere corpus, et undis
translation.text:
To whom once more the winged god appears; His former youthful mien and shape he wears, And with this new alarm invades his ears: “Sleep’st thou, O goddess-born! and canst thou drown Thy needful cares, so near a hostile town, Beset with foes; nor hear’st the western gales Invite thy passage, and inspire thy sails? She harbours in her heart a furious hate, And thou shalt find the dire effects too late; Fix’d on revenge, and obstinate to die. Haste swiftly hence, while thou hast pow’r to fly. The sea with ships will soon be cover’d o’er, And blazing firebrands kindle all the shore. Prevent her rage, while night obscures the skies, And sail before the purple morn arise. Who knows what hazards thy delay may bring? Woman’s a various and a changeful thing.” Thus Hermes in the dream; then took his flight Aloft in air unseen, and mix’d with night.

Twice warn’d by the celestial messenger, The pious prince arose with hasty fear; Then rous’d his drowsy train without delay: “Haste to your banks; your crooked anchors weigh, And spread your flying sails, and stand to sea. A god commands: he stood before my sight, And urg’d us once again to speedy flight. O sacred pow’r, what pow’r soe’er thou art, To thy blest orders I resign my heart. Lead thou the way; protect thy Trojan bands, And prosper the design thy will commands.” He said: and, drawing forth his flaming sword, His thund’ring arm divides the many-twisted cord. An emulating zeal inspires his train: They run; they snatch; they rush into the main. With headlong haste they leave the desert shores, And brush the liquid seas with lab’ring oars.

translation.original_text: <sup>601</sup>spargere? Non socios, non ipsum absumere ferro <sup>602</sup>Ascanium, patriisque epulandum ponere mensis?— <sup>603</sup>Verum anceps pugnae fuerat fortuna:—fuisset. <sup>604</sup>Quem metui moritura? Faces in castra tulissem, <sup>605</sup>implessemque foros flammis, natumque patremque <sup>606</sup>cum genere extinxem, memet super ipsa dedissem. <sup>607</sup>Sol, qui terrarum flammis opera omnia lustras, <sup>608</sup>tuque harum interpres curarum et conscia Iuno, <sup>609</sup>nocturnisque Hecate triviis ululata per urbes, <sup>610</sup>et Dirae ultrices, et di morientis Elissae, <sup>611</sup>accipite haec, meritumque malis advertite numen, <sup>612</sup>et nostras audite preces. Si tangere portus <sup>613</sup>infandum caput ac terris adnare necesse est, <sup>614</sup>et sic fata Iovis poscunt, hic terminus haeret: <sup>615</sup>at bello audacis populi vexatus et armis, <sup>616</sup>finibus extorris, complexu avulsus Iuli, <sup>617</sup>auxilium imploret, videatque indigna suorum <sup>618</sup>funera; nec, cum se sub leges pacis iniquae <sup>619</sup>tradiderit, regno aut optata luce fruatur, <sup>620</sup>sed cadat ante diem, mediaque inhumatus harena. <sup>621</sup>Haec precor, hanc vocem extremam cum sanguine fundo. <sup>622</sup>Tum vos, o Tyrii, stirpem et genus omne futurum <sup>623</sup>exercete odiis, cinerique haec mittite nostro <sup>624</sup>munera. Nullus amor populis, nec foedera sunto. <sup>625</sup>Exoriare aliquis nostris ex ossibus ultor,
translation.text:
Aurora now had left her saffron bed, And beams of early light the heav’ns o’erspread, When, from a tow’r, the queen, with wakeful eyes, Saw day point upward from the rosy skies. She look’d to seaward; but the sea was void, And scarce in ken the sailing ships descried. Stung with despite, and furious with despair, She struck her trembling breast, and tore her hair. “And shall th’ ungrateful traitor go,” she said, “My land forsaken, and my love betray’d? Shall we not arm? not rush from ev’ry street, To follow, sink, and burn his perjur’d fleet? Haste, haul my galleys out! pursue the foe! Bring flaming brands! set sail, and swiftly row! What have I said? where am I? Fury turns My brain; and my distemper’d bosom burns. Then, when I gave my person and my throne, This hate, this rage, had been more timely shown. See now the promis’d faith, the vaunted name, The pious man, who, rushing thro’ the flame, Preserv’d his gods, and to the Phrygian shore The burthen of his feeble father bore! I should have torn him piecemeal; strow’d in floods His scatter’d limbs, or left expos’d in woods; Destroy’d his friends and son; and, from the fire, Have set the reeking boy before the sire. Events are doubtful, which on battles wait: Yet where’s the doubt, to souls secure of fate? My Tyrians, at their injur’d queen’s command, Had toss’d their fires amid the Trojan band; At once extinguish’d all the faithless name; And I myself, in vengeance of my shame, Had fall’n upon the pile, to mend the fun’ral flame. Thou Sun, who view’st at once the world below; Thou Juno, guardian of the nuptial vow; Thou Hecate hearken from thy dark abodes! Ye Furies, fiends, and violated gods, All pow’rs invok’d with Dido’s dying breath, Attend her curses and avenge her death! If so the Fates ordain, Jove commands, Th’ ungrateful wretch should find the Latian lands, Yet let a race untam’d, and haughty foes, His peaceful entrance with dire arms oppose: Oppress’d with numbers in th’ unequal field, His men discourag’d, and himself expell’d, Let him for succour sue from place to place, Torn from his subjects, and his son’s embrace. First, let him see his friends in battle slain, And their untimely fate lament in vain; And when, at length, the cruel war shall cease, On hard conditions may he buy his peace: Nor let him then enjoy supreme command; But fall, untimely, by some hostile hand, And lie unburied on the barren sand! These are my pray’rs, and this my dying will; And you, my Tyrians, ev’ry curse fulfil. Perpetual hate and mortal wars proclaim, Against the prince, the people, and the name. These grateful off’rings on my grave bestow; Nor league, nor love, the hostile nations know! Now, and from hence, in ev’ry future age, When rage excites your arms, and strength supplies the rage Rise some avenger of our Libyan blood, With fire and sword pursue the perjur’d brood; Our arms, our seas, our shores, oppos’d to theirs; And the same hate descend on all our heirs!”

This said, within her anxious mind she weighs The means of cutting short her odious days. Then to Sichaeus’ nurse she briefly said (For, when she left her country, hers was dead): “Go, Barce, call my sister.
