Let her care The solemn rites of sacrifice prepare; The sheep, and all th’ atoning off’rings bring, Sprinkling her body from the crystal spring With living drops; then let her come, and thou With sacred fillets bind thy hoary brow. Thus will I pay my vows to Stygian Jove, And end the cares of my disastrous love; Then cast the Trojan image on the fire, And, as that burns, my passions shall expire.”

translation.original_text: <sup>626</sup>qui face Dardanios ferroque sequare colonos, <sup>627</sup>nunc, olim, quocumque dabunt se tempore vires. <sup>628</sup>Litora litoribus contraria, fluctibus undas <sup>629</sup>imprecor, arma armis; pugnent ipsique nepotesque. <sup>630</sup>Haec ait, et partis animum versabat in omnis, <sup>631</sup>invisam quaerens quam primum abrumpere lucem. <sup>632</sup>Tum breviter Barcen nutricem adfata Sychaei; <sup>633</sup>namque suam patria antiqua cinis ater habebat: <sup>634</sup>Annam cara mihi nutrix huc siste sororem; <sup>635</sup>dic corpus properet fluviali spargere lympha, <sup>636</sup>et pecudes secum et monstrata piacula ducat: <sup>637</sup>sic veniat; tuque ipsa pia tege tempora vitta. <sup>638</sup>Sacra Iovi Stygio, quae rite incepta paravi, <sup>639</sup>perficere est animus, finemque imponere curis, <sup>640</sup>Dardaniique rogum capitis permittere flammae. <sup>641</sup>Sic ait: illa gradum studio celerabat anili. <sup>642</sup>At trepida, et coeptis immanibus effera Dido, <sup>643</sup>sanguineam volvens aciem, maculisque trementis <sup>644</sup>interfusa genas, et pallida morte futura, <sup>645</sup>interiora domus inrumpit limina, et altos <sup>646</sup>conscendit furibunda rogos, ensemque recludit <sup>647</sup>Dardanium, non hos quaesitum munus in usus. <sup>648</sup>Hic, postquam Iliacas vestes notumque cubile <sup>649</sup>conspexit, paulum lacrimis et mente morata, <sup>650</sup>incubuitque toro, dixitque novissima verba:
translation.text: The nurse moves onward, with officious care, And all the speed her aged limbs can bear. But furious Dido, with dark thoughts involv’d, Shook at the mighty mischief she resolv’d. With livid spots distinguish’d was her face; Red were her rolling eyes, and discompos’d her pace; Ghastly she gaz’d, with pain she drew her breath, And nature shiver’d at approaching death.

translation.original_text: <sup>651</sup>Dulces exuviae, dum fata deusque sinebant, <sup>652</sup>accipite hanc animam, meque his exsolvite curis. <sup>653</sup>Vixi, et, quem dederat cursum fortuna, peregi, <sup>654</sup>et nunc magna mei sub terras ibit imago. <sup>655</sup>Urbem praeclaram statui; mea moenia vidi; <sup>656</sup>ulta virum, poenas inimico a fratre recepi; <sup>657</sup>felix, heu nimium felix, si litora tantum <sup>658</sup>numquam Dardaniae tetigissent nostra carinae! <sup>659</sup>Dixit, et, os impressa toro, Moriemur inultae, <sup>660</sup>sed moriamur ait. Sic, sic iuvat ire sub umbras: <sup>661</sup>Hauriat hunc oculis ignem crudelis ab alto <sup>662</sup>Dardanus, et nostrae secum ferat omina mortis. <sup>663</sup>Dixerat; atque illam media inter talia ferro <sup>664</sup>conlapsam aspiciunt comites, ensemque cruore <sup>665</sup>spumantem, sparsasque manus. It clamor ad alta <sup>666</sup>atria; concussam bacchatur Fama per urbem. <sup>667</sup>Lamentis gemituque et femineo ululatu <sup>668</sup>tecta fremunt; resonat magnis plangoribus aether, <sup>669</sup>non aliter, quam si immissis ruat hostibus omnis <sup>670</sup>Karthago aut antiqua Tyros, flammaeque furentes <sup>671</sup>culmina perque hominum volvantur perque deorum. <sup>672</sup>Audiit exanimis, trepidoque exterrita cursu <sup>673</sup>unguibus ora soror foedans et pectora pugnis <sup>674</sup>per medios ruit, ac morientem nomine clamat: <sup>675</sup>Hoc illud, germana, fuit? Me fraude petebas?
translation.text:
Then swiftly to the fatal place she pass’d, And mounts the fun’ral pile with furious haste; Unsheathes the sword the Trojan left behind (Not for so dire an enterprise design’d). But when she view’d the garments loosely spread, Which once he wore, and saw the conscious bed, She paus’d, and with a sigh the robes embrac’d; Then on the couch her trembling body cast, Repress’d the ready tears, and spoke her last: “Dear pledges of my love, while Heav’n so pleas’d, Receive a soul, of mortal anguish eas’d: My fatal course is finish’d; and I go, A glorious name, among the ghosts below. A lofty city by my hands is rais’d, Pygmalion punish’d, and my lord appeas’d. What could my fortune have afforded more, Had the false Trojan never touch’d my shore!” Then kiss’d the couch; and, “Must I die,” she said, “And unreveng’d? ’Tis doubly to be dead! Yet ev’n this death with pleasure I receive: On any terms, ’tis better than to live. These flames, from far, may the false Trojan view; These boding omens his base flight pursue!”

She said, and struck; deep enter’d in her side The piercing steel, with reeking purple dyed: Clogg’d in the wound the cruel weapon stands; The spouting blood came streaming on her hands. Her sad attendants saw the deadly stroke, And with loud cries the sounding palace shook. Distracted, from the fatal sight they fled, And thro’ the town the dismal rumour spread. First from the frighted court the yell began; Redoubled, thence from house to house it ran: The groans of men, with shrieks, laments, and cries Of mixing women, mount the vaulted skies. Not less the clamour, than if ancient Tyre, Or the new Carthage, set by foes on fire, The rolling ruin, with their lov’d abodes, Involv’d the blazing temples of their gods.

translation.original_text: <sup>676</sup>Hoc rogus iste mihi, hoc ignes araeque parabant? <sup>677</sup>Quid primum deserta querar? Comitemne sororem <sup>678</sup>sprevisti moriens? Eadem me ad fata vocasses: <sup>679</sup>idem ambas ferro dolor, atque eadem hora tulisset. <sup>680</sup>His etiam struxi manibus, patriosque vocavi <sup>681</sup>voce deos, sic te ut posita crudelis abessem? <sup>682</sup>Exstinxti te meque, soror, populumque patresque <sup>683</sup>Sidonios urbemque tuam. Date volnera lymphis <sup>684</sup>abluam, et, extremus si quis super halitus errat, <sup>685</sup>ore legam. Sic fata, gradus evaserat altos, <sup>686</sup>semianimemque sinu germanam amplexa fovebat <sup>687</sup>cum gemitu, atque atros siccabat veste cruores. <sup>688</sup>Illa, graves oculos conata attollere, rursus <sup>689</sup>deficit; infixum stridit sub pectore vulnus. <sup>690</sup>Ter sese attollens cubitoque adnixa levavit; <sup>691</sup>ter revoluta toro est, oculisque errantibus alto <sup>692</sup>quaesivit caelo lucem, ingemuitque reperta. <sup>693</sup>Tum Iuno omnipotens, longum miserata dolorem <sup>694</sup>difficilisque obitus, Irim demisit Olympo, <sup>695</sup>quae luctantem animam nexosque resolveret artus. <sup>696</sup>Nam quia nec fato, merita nec morte peribat, <sup>697</sup>sed misera ante diem, subitoque accensa furore, <sup>698</sup>nondum illi flavum Proserpina vertice crinem <sup>699</sup>abstulerat, Stygioque caput damnaverat Orco. <sup>700</sup>Ergo Iris croceis per caelum roscida pennis,
translation.text: Her sister hears; and, furious with despair, She beats her breast, and rends her yellow hair, And, calling on Eliza’s name aloud, Runs breathless to the place, and breaks the crowd. “Was all that pomp of woe for this prepar’d; These fires, this fun’ral pile, these altars rear’d? Was all this train of plots contriv’d,” said she, “All only to deceive unhappy me? Which is the worst? Didst thou in death pretend To scorn thy sister, or delude thy friend? Thy summon’d sister, and thy friend, had come; One sword had serv’d us both, one common tomb: Was I to raise the pile, the pow’rs invoke, Not to be present at the fatal stroke? At once thou hast destroy’d thyself and me, Thy town, thy senate, and thy colony! Bring water; bathe the wound; while I in death Lay close my lips to hers, and catch the flying breath.” This said, she mounts the pile with eager haste, And in her arms the gasping queen embrac’d; Her temples chaf’d; and her own garments tore, To stanch the streaming blood, and cleanse the gore. Thrice Dido tried to raise her drooping head, And, fainting thrice, fell grov’ling on the bed; Thrice op’d her heavy eyes, and sought the light, But, having found it, sicken’d at the sight, And clos’d her lids at last in endless night.

translation.original_text: <sup>701</sup>mille trahens varios adverso sole colores, <sup>702</sup>devolat, et supra caput adstitit: Hunc ego Diti <sup>703</sup>sacrum iussa fero, teque isto corpore solvo. <sup>704</sup>Sic ait, et dextra crinem secat: omnis et una <sup>705</sup>dilapsus calor, atque in ventos vita recessit.
translation.text: Then Juno, grieving that she should sustain A death so ling’ring, and so full of pain, Sent Iris down, to free her from the strife Of lab’ring nature, and dissolve her life. For since she died, not doom’d by Heav’n’s decree, Or her own crime, but human casualty, And rage of love, that plung’d her in despair, The Sisters had not cut the topmost hair, Which Proserpine and they can only know; Nor made her sacred to the shades below. Downward the various goddess took her flight, And drew a thousand colours from the light; Then stood above the dying lover’s head, And said: “I thus devote thee to the dead. This off’ring to th’ infernal gods I bear.” Thus while she spoke, she cut the fatal hair: The struggling soul was loos’d, and life dissolv’d in air.
